revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Tier 2]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Aichi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category: 6]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[ja:Masumida Shrine]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Aichi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category: 6]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[ja:Masumida Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Aichi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category: 6]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[ja:Masumida Shrine]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Original templates]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Aichi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category: 6]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masumida Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[ja:Masumida Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masumida Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Aichi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category: 6]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[ja:Masumida Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the Rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Aichi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[ja:Masumida Shrine]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Ja import failed]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the Rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Aichi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masumida Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[sn:asumida Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the Rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Aichi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[sn:asumida Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Meiji restoration]] → [[Meiji Restoration]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Masumida Shrine
真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Owari Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Masumida Jinja is: * {{nihongo| '''[[Amenohoakari|Amenoho no Akari no mikoto]]''' | 天火明命}}, ''kami'' of the sun and agriculture, the younger brother (or father) of [[Ninigi]], and the ancestor of the Owari clan, the prehistoric rulers of the area. ==History== The date of Masumida Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition and the ''[[Kujiki]]'' records give the unlikely date of 628 BC, or the third day of the third month of the 33rd year in the reign of [[Emperor Jimmu]], when the spirit of Amenohoakari was brought to Owari from the [[Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Mount Katsuragi]] in [[Yamato Province]]. Another tradition gives the date of foundation to the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Suinin]] (97 BC – 30 BC). The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of [[Owari Province]], established in the [[Nara period]] and features in the [[Yamato Takeru]] myth cycle. During the early [[Heian period]], it appears in the ''[[Rikkokushi]]'' and in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled as the ''ichinomiya'' of Owari Province since at least the end of the Heian period. In 1584, after the shrine was damaged by an earthquake, it was rebuilt by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was subsequently supported by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and [[Owari Domain]] until the end of the [[Edo period]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was given the Rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei shosha}} in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1885. It was promoted a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|Kokuheii chusha}} in 1914.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine was destroyed in the [[Bombing of Ichinomiya in World War II|Ichinomiya air raid]] of 1945 and was not rebuilt until 1951, with reconstruction taking ten years.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located ten-minutes on foot from either [[Owari-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] or [[Meitetsu Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Nagoya Main Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Masumida Shrine - Romon.jpg|''Rōmon'' Masumida Shrine - Torii and Romon.jpg|Torii and ''Rōmon'' Masumida-jinja (Ichinomiya) honden.JPG|''Honden'' ==Cultural properties== ===[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Bugaku]]'' masks'''|木造舞楽面|Mokuzō bugaku-men}}, set of 12, ten from the [[Kamakura period]], two from the [[Muromachi period]], donation from [[Juntoku Tenno]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3033|title=木造舞楽面〈陵王一、納曽利一、還城楽一、／崑崙八仙三、童舞二、二ノ舞二、／貴徳一、散手一、〉 |trans-title=Mokuzō bugaku-men|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Red Lacquerware''|朱漆角切盤/朱漆入角盤/朱漆擎子 附(銅鋺/銅皿)}}, set of 25 items, including lacquer trays, saucers and bowls, dated 1457{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/7281|title=朱漆角切盤／朱漆入角盤／朱漆擎子｝ |trans-title=Shu urushi Tsunokiri-ban/ Shu urushi Iri Kakuban|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ===[[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)#Registered Tangible Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Properties]]=== *{{nihongo| '''''[[Honden]]'' and connecting building'''|真清田神社本殿及び渡殿|Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono}} {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00005618 |title=真清田神社本殿及び渡殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja honden oyobi watadono|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''North Gate and Wall'''|真清田神社北門及び透塀|Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006768 |title=真清田神社北門及び透塀 |trans-title=Masumida jinja kitamon oyobi Tōru hei|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''Ceremonial Library'''l|真清田神社祭文殿 |Masumida jinja saimonden}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00006769 |title=真清田神社祭文殿 |trans-title=Masumida jinja saimonden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]].{{ISBN|1-873-41063-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Masumida-jinja, Ichinomiya|Masumida Shrine}} *{{Official|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} {{in lang|ja}} * [https://www.aichi-now.jp/en/spots/detail/302/ Aichi Prefecture tourist information] ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Owari Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Owari clan]] }} [[sn:Masumida Shrine]] [[en:Masumida Shrine]] [[sn:asumida Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

真清田神社 | image = Masumida Shrine Haiden.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Masumida Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|18|27|N|136|48|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenohoakari]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 2-1 Masumida 1-chōme, Ichinomiya, Aichi 491-0043 | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.masumida.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Masumida Shrine'''|真清田神社|Masumida Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Masumida neighborhood of the city of [[Ichinomiya, Aichi|Ichinomiya]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], [[Japan]].
